mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 5 Series 550i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 550i V8 engine typically costs £2,500–£6,500 supply only depending on which generation you have (E60's N62 is cheaper; F10/G30's N63 is more expensive). Fitting adds £1,200–£2,200. A full engine replacement with labour can cost £9,000–£14,000 for premium vehicles. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 5 Series 550i engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which generation your 550i has. The E60's N62 is a silky naturally aspirated V8 with a well‑documented weakness: valve stem seals. The F10's N63 gained a notorious reputation for timing chain issues, excessive oil consumption, and heat‑soak problems. The G30's N63B44M3 is the most reliable of the family, with successive TU revisions significantly improving durability.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 5 Series 550i?

acceptedAnswer.text: On the E60 (N62), the most common issue is valve stem seal failure causing blue smoke and excessive oil consumption. On the F10 (N63), the most serious issues are timing chain stretch and guide failure, excessive oil consumption (0.5–1 litre per 1,000 km), injector failure, and cooling system/heat‑soak problems. The G30's N63B44M3 is significantly improved.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a 550i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a tidy £5,000–£15,000 550i, a £3,000–£6,500 recon is 20–65% of the car's value – often worth it on well‑maintained examples. If the timing chain has snapped on an N63, a full engine replacement may be necessary. Get a full diagnostic assessment before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my 5 Series's engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The E60 (2005–2010) uses the N62B48 – check for a 4.8L naturally aspirated V8. The F10 (2010–2016) uses N63 variants – check for a 4.4L twin‑turbo V8. The G30 (2017–present) uses the N63B44M3 – check for the M550i badge. The engine code is stamped on the engine block and listed on your V5C.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair a 550i engine issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most issues – N62 valve stem seals, N63 timing chain wear (if caught early), oil consumption, injectors, cooling system – are repairable. However, if the timing chain has snapped on an N63, the engine is an interference design – pistons will strike valves. In this case, a full engine replacement is usually more cost‑effective than a rebuild.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the 550i pricing across generations. The E60's N62 is generally cheaper to replace (£2,500–£4,200); the F10's N63 and G30's N63B44M3 command a premium (£3,500–£6,500). A full engine replacement with labour can cost £9,000–£14,000 for premium vehicles.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a 550i V8 engine typically takes 14–22 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent BMW specialist). The N63's hot‑vee design adds significant labour complexity.

problems.title: N62 Valve Stem Seal Failure (E60, 2005–2010)
problems.symptoms: Blue smoke from exhaust on start-up and after idling, excessive oil consumption (1 litre per 1,000 miles or more), fouled spark plugs, failed emissions test.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored oil consumption can damage catalytic converters).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you notice blue smoke or excessive oil consumption.

problems.recommendation: The N62's valve stem seals are the defining weakness of this otherwise robust engine. Over time, the seals harden and allow oil to enter the combustion chamber. Symptoms include blue smoke on start-up and after idling, and oil consumption that can reach 1 litre per 1,000 miles. The fix is labour‑intensive — the cylinder heads must be removed, making this a £2,000–£4,000 job. However, if caught early, the engine itself is otherwise durable. As one specialist noted, "the unreliability is not breakdown/catastrophic type unreliability it's often leaks, both oil and coolant and the valve stem seals".

problems.title: N63 Timing Chain Stretch & Guide Failure (F10, 2010–2016)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise during cold starts, metallic whine from the engine, engine warning light (cam timing faults), loss of power — catastrophic failure can occur without warning.
problems.replacementCost: £3,500–£6,500 [PLACEHOLDER] (if chain snaps and damages the engine).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling on cold start, book an inspection immediately.

problems.recommendation: The N63's plastic timing chain guides can become brittle and fail, causing chain stretch. BMW even launched a Customer Care Package program to address widespread issues. As one source noted, "many N63 motors will see timing chain rattle past 100,000 miles". A £2,500–£5,000 preventative chain replacement is far cheaper than a £3,500–£6,500 engine replacement. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, have the timing system inspected immediately. The N63 has been described as "the most unreliable BMW engine ever made" by some owners.

problems.title: N63 Excessive Oil Consumption & Injector Failure (F10, 2010–2016)
problems.symptoms: Oil consumption of 0.5–1 litre per 1,000 km, blue smoke from exhaust, rough idle, misfires, engine warning light, failed emissions test.
problems.typicalMileage: 30,000–60,000 miles (can occur at relatively low mileage).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored injector failure can damage catalytic converters).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you notice excessive oil consumption or misfires.

problems.recommendation: The early N63 engines are notorious for excessive oil consumption — up to 1 litre per 1,000 km. This is often due to piston ring wear or oil control issues. Fuel injector failure is also common, with failing injectors causing misfires and rough running. The N63TU (2013+) addressed some of these issues, but oil consumption remains a concern. Regular oil level checks and top‑ups are essential — running low on oil can cause catastrophic engine failure.

problems.title: N63 Cooling System & Heat‑Soak Issues (F10, 2010–2016)
problems.symptoms: Overheating, coolant loss, engine warning light, reduced power on hot days, turbocharger issues.
problems.repairCost: £500–£2,000 [PLACEHOLDER] (cooling system overhaul, radiator, water pump, turbo coolant lines).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored overheating can cause head gasket failure).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if overheating occurs.

problems.recommendation: The N63's hot‑vee design places the turbochargers in the V of the engine, which creates significant heat‑soak issues. Coolant lines to the turbos can fail, and the engine's cooling system is under constant stress. Regular coolant changes and inspections of the cooling system are essential. If you notice any overheating, stop driving immediately — aluminium V8s do not tolerate overheating well.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: BMW 5 Series 550i — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Labour‑intensive but cheaper than engine replacement
Catch it early — cheaper than engine replacement
Full engine replacement — valve damage is almost certain
Worth fixing — common and well‑understood repairs
Full engine replacement often more cost‑effective

buyingChecks.title: Buying This BMW 5 Series 550i? Check These First
buyIf: * Full BMW service history with documented oil changes (every 8,000–10,000 miles)
* E60 (N62): Valve stem seals replaced — this is the defining weakness
* F10 (N63): Timing chain inspected and BMW Customer Care Package completed
* F10 (N63): Injectors replaced with updated parts and oil consumption addressed
* G30 (N63B44M3): The most reliable of the N63 family — latest TU3 revision
avoidIf: * E60 (N62): Any sign of blue smoke or excessive oil consumption
* F10 (N63): Any sign of cold‑start rattle — this is the defining weakness
* F10 (N63): No evidence of timing chain inspection or Customer Care Package
* Any sign of overheating or coolant loss
* No evidence of regular oil changes — all 550i V8s are intolerant of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The BMW 550i badge represents the pinnacle of V8-powered 5 Series motoring. The E60's N62 is a silky naturally aspirated V8 with a single, well‑documented weakness: valve stem seals. The F10's N63 is a powerful twin‑turbo V8 that gained a notorious reputation for timing chain issues, oil consumption, and heat‑soak problems — BMW even launched a Customer Care Package to address widespread issues. The G30's N63B44M3 is the most reliable of the family, with BMW having "worked on the errors" through successive TU revisions. Buy on service history above all else — a well‑maintained 550i of any generation is a rewarding and powerful car; a neglected example can be a financial black hole.

cta.label: → Compare 5 Series 550i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 5 Series 550i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Any rattle or whine? (N63 timing chain warning — stop immediately)
2. Blue Smoke — Any smoke on start-up or after idling? (N62 valve stem seals)
3. Service History — Regular oil changes (8,000–10,000 miles) — this is non‑negotiable
4. Timing Chain History — F10 (N63): Has the chain been inspected or replaced? Was the Customer Care Package completed?
5. Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles? (N63 warning sign)
6. Cooling System — Any overheating history? Coolant leaks? (N63 heat‑soak issues)
7. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any timing, injector, or misfire codes?
8. MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures or any advisory notes

items.label: SECTION 8 — FAQ
items.value: 1. How much does a BMW 5 Series 550i engine replacement cost in the UK?

items.label: A reconditioned 550i V8 engine typically costs £2,500–£6,500 supply only depending on which generation you have (E60's N62 is cheaper; F10/G30's N63 is more expensive). Fitting adds £1,200–£2,200. A full engine replacement with labour can cost £9,000–£14,000 for premium vehicles. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.
items.value: 2. Is the BMW 5 Series 550i engine reliable?

items.label: It depends on which generation your 550i has. The E60's N62 is a silky naturally aspirated V8 with a well‑documented weakness: valve stem seals. The F10's N63 gained a notorious reputation for timing chain issues, excessive oil consumption, and heat‑soak problems. The G30's N63B44M3 is the most reliable of the family, with successive TU revisions significantly improving durability.
items.value: 3. What are the common problems with the BMW 5 Series 550i?

items.label: On the E60 (N62), the most common issue is valve stem seal failure causing blue smoke and excessive oil consumption. On the F10 (N63), the most serious issues are timing chain stretch and guide failure, excessive oil consumption (0.5–1 litre per 1,000 km), injector failure, and cooling system/heat‑soak problems. The G30's N63B44M3 is significantly improved.
items.value: 4. Is it worth replacing a 550i engine, or should I scrap the car?

items.label: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a tidy £5,000–£15,000 550i, a £3,000–£6,500 recon is 20–65% of the car's value — often worth it on well‑maintained examples. If the timing chain has snapped on an N63, a full engine replacement may be necessary. Get a full diagnostic assessment before committing.
items.value: 5. How do I find my 5 Series's engine code?

items.label: The E60 (2005–2010) uses the N62B48 — check for a 4.8L naturally aspirated V8. The F10 (2010–2016) uses N63 variants — check for a 4.4L twin‑turbo V8. The G30 (2017–present) uses the N63B44M3 — check for the M550i badge. The engine code is stamped on the engine block and listed on your V5C.
items.value: 6. Can I repair a 550i engine issue, or does it need replacement?

items.label: Most issues — N62 valve stem seals, N63 timing chain wear (if caught early), oil consumption, injectors, cooling system — are repairable. However, if the timing chain has snapped on an N63, the engine is an interference design — pistons will strike valves. In this case, a full engine replacement is usually more cost‑effective than a rebuild.
